title: Equity, poverty & income distribution
summary:
## The big idea Markets are good at creating income but indifferent about who gets it. Whether the resulting distribution is *fair* — and what, if anything, the state should do about it — is where economics meets politics head-on. This chapter gives you the measuring instruments (**Lorenz curve, Gini coefficient**), the vocabulary (**equity vs equality, absolute vs relative poverty**), and the policy toolkit — plus the tension that powers every evaluation: redistributing the pie can shrink it. ## Equity is not equality - **Equality** = everyone gets the *same*. **Equity** = the distribution is *fair* — a **normative** judgement that may tolerate (even require) inequality: most people think a surgeon fairly earns more than a student. - **Horizontal equity** — identical people treated identically (two workers with the same income pay the same tax). - **Vertical equity** — different people treated appropriately differently (the rich pay proportionally more). And the framing tension of the whole topic — the **equity–efficiency trade-off**: heavy redistribution can dull incentives to work, save, invest and take risks; but extreme inequality wastes talent (the poor can't fund education), corrodes social cohesion, and can itself slow growth. Where the balance lies is a value judgement — say so in essays. ## Measuring the distribution First, keep two concepts apart: **income** is a *flow* (wages, interest, benefits per year); **wealth** is a *stock* (houses, shares, land owned at a moment). Wealth is *always* more unequally distributed than income — it accumulates and it's inherited.
**What each part means:** rank the population from poorest to richest along the bottom, then plot what share of total income each cumulative slice earns. Perfect equality would trace the **yellow 45° line** — the poorest 40% would earn exactly 40% of income. The **red Lorenz curve** shows reality: it sags below, because the poorest 40% might earn only 15%. The further it bows, the greater the inequality. The **Gini coefficient** compresses the picture into one number: the shaded **area A** (between equality line and Lorenz curve) divided by the whole triangle **A + B**. **Gini = 0** → perfect equality; **Gini = 1** → one person has everything. Real countries run from ~0.25 (Scandinavia) to ~0.63 (South Africa).
## Poverty — two very different definitions - **Absolute poverty** — income below what's needed for **basic physical necessities** (food, shelter, clothing); the World Bank's extreme-poverty line (a few dollars a day at PPP) is the global benchmark. Economic growth *can* eliminate it — and in East Asia largely has. - **Relative poverty** — income below some fraction (commonly **60% of the median**) of what's normal *in that society*. It measures exclusion rather than starvation — and because it's pegged to the median, it can persist (or worsen) while a country grows richer. A rich country abolishes absolute poverty long before relative poverty. **Where poverty comes from:** unemployment, low pay and low skills, ill-health, disability and old age, family breakdown, discrimination — and the **poverty trap**: when earning an extra dollar costs nearly a dollar in withdrawn benefits and taxes, the *effective* marginal tax rate on the poor approaches 100% and working more becomes irrational. Traps are a *design failure* of welfare systems, not a character failure of the poor. ## The policy toolkit - **Progressive taxation** — average tax rate *rises* with income (via rising marginal rates and allowances). Contrast **proportional** (flat rate) and **regressive** taxes — VAT and excise duties take a *bigger share* of a poor household's income. A tax *system's* overall progressivity depends on the mix. - **Transfer payments** — pensions, unemployment, child and disability benefits. **Universal** benefits are simple, stigma-free, high take-up — but expensive and poorly targeted; **means-tested** benefits are cheap and targeted — but administratively heavy, stigmatising, incompletely claimed, and the very source of poverty traps. - **State provision of merit goods** — free education and healthcare attack the *transmission* of poverty across generations by building human capital. - **Minimum wages** — raise the lowest pay (with the labour-market caveats from Unit 7). - **Supply-side approaches** — training, education, childcare, regional policy: tackle the **causes** (low productivity) rather than the **symptoms** (low income) — slower but more durable. **Evaluating any package:** cost and opportunity cost; incentive effects (both the trade-off *and* its limits — modest redistribution has small measured effects on effort); targeting vs take-up; and the underlying **normative** choice about how much inequality a society will accept. Strong answers separate *symptom-relief* (transfers) from *cause-treatment* (education) and note the time horizons differ. ## Why it matters Equity supplies the 'fairness' half of almost every evaluation — taxes, minimum wages, privatisation, development. And the Lorenz/Gini apparatus is a Paper 3 favourite: know the axes, the 0–1 scale, and what shifts the curve.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Equity** (fairness, normative) ≠ **equality** (sameness). **Horizontal** vs **vertical** equity. - **Income = flow; wealth = stock** — wealth always more unequal. - **Lorenz curve**: cumulative income vs cumulative population; bow = inequality. **Gini = A/(A+B)**: 0 equality → 1 maximal. - **Absolute poverty** (basic needs; growth can end it) vs **relative** (share of median; can persist forever). - **Poverty trap**: benefit withdrawal + tax ≈ 100% effective marginal rate → work doesn't pay. - Tools: **progressive tax** (vs proportional/regressive), transfers (**universal vs means-tested** trade-off), merit-good provision, minimum wage, supply-side (causes not symptoms). - Frame evaluations with the **equity–efficiency trade-off** — and flag the normative judgement.

keyTerms.definition: Equity is fairness (which may be unequal); equality is everyone having the same.
Treating people in identical circumstances in the same way.
Treating people in different circumstances differently (e.g. the rich pay more tax).
A graph of cumulative income share against cumulative population share, showing inequality.
A 0–1 measure of inequality; 0 is perfect equality, 1 is perfect inequality.
Income too low to afford basic necessities.
Income below a set proportion of the median in a society.
A tax taking a larger proportion of income as income rises.
When earning more income leads to lost benefits/higher tax, weakening the incentive to work.

diagrams: **Lorenz curve:** the 45° line of equality and a bowed Lorenz curve; the more it bows, the greater the inequality; relate the gap to the Gini coefficient.
Optional: a tax-incidence sketch contrasting **progressive, proportional and regressive** taxes.
tips: Distinguish **income (flow) from wealth (stock)** and note wealth is usually more unequal.
Explain the **Lorenz curve and Gini coefficient** precisely (0–1 scale) — common data-response tools.
Separate **absolute vs relative poverty** and note relative poverty can persist despite growth.
Frame answers around the **equity–efficiency trade-off** and the **poverty/unemployment trap** for evaluation.
Classify policies as tackling **symptoms (transfers) vs causes (education/supply-side)** — a strong structuring point.

channels.why: Clear on inequality, Lorenz/Gini and poverty policy.

keyConcepts.title: Poverty and policies to reduce it

revisionNotes:
# Equity, poverty & income distribution — quick revision

**Concepts**
- Equity = fairness (normative); equality = sameness. Horizontal (same treated same) vs vertical (different treated differently).
- Income = **flow**; wealth = **stock** (wealth more unequal — accumulation + inheritance).
- **Equity–efficiency trade-off**: redistribution can blunt incentives; extreme inequality also harms growth/cohesion.

**Measurement**
- **Lorenz curve**: cumulative % income (y) vs cumulative % population (x), poorest first; sag below 45° line = inequality.
- **Gini = A/(A+B)**: 0 = perfect equality, 1 = perfect inequality (~0.25 Scandinavia → ~0.63 South Africa).

**Poverty**
- **Absolute**: below basic-needs line (World Bank) — growth can eliminate.
- **Relative**: below ~60% of median — tracks exclusion; persists despite growth.
- Causes: unemployment, low skills/pay, illness/age, discrimination + **poverty trap** (benefit withdrawal → ~100% effective marginal tax rate).

**Policies**
- **Progressive tax** (rising average rate) vs proportional vs **regressive** (VAT).
- Transfers: **universal** (simple, costly, untargeted) vs **means-tested** (targeted, stigma, take-up, traps).
- Merit-good provision (education/health = human capital = attacks causes).
- Minimum wage (labour-market caveats), supply-side (training, childcare).

**Evaluation frame** — symptoms (transfers, fast) vs causes (education, slow); cost; incentives; normative balance.
questions:
# Practice questions — Equity, poverty & income distribution **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** A Gini coefficient that falls from 0.48 to 0.39 indicates that: - A — income inequality has increased - B — income inequality has decreased - C — average income has fallen - D — absolute poverty has been eliminated
▸ Show answer

B. The Gini runs from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality); a fall means the Lorenz curve has moved toward the line of equality. It says nothing about income levels or absolute poverty.

**2.** Which statement about income and wealth is correct? - A — income is a stock and wealth is a flow - B — income is a flow and wealth is a stock, and wealth is usually more unequally distributed - C — both are flows - D — wealth is always distributed more equally than income
▸ Show answer

B. Income arrives per period (flow); wealth is what's owned at a point in time (stock) — and accumulation plus inheritance makes wealth far more unequal.

**3.** A sales tax of 14% on food is regressive because: - A — the tax rate rises with income - B — poorer households spend a larger proportion of their income on food - C — rich households buy more food - D — food is a merit good
▸ Show answer

B. A flat-rate tax on a necessity takes a bigger share of a poor household's income than a rich one's — the definition of regressive incidence.

**4.** A country's real GDP per head doubles over two decades, yet the share of people below 60% of median income is unchanged. This shows that: - A — absolute poverty has not fallen - B — relative poverty can persist despite economic growth - C — the Gini coefficient must have risen - D — the Lorenz curve has moved inward
▸ Show answer

B. Relative poverty is pegged to the median: if all incomes double, the 60%-of-median line doubles too. Growth typically cuts absolute poverty while leaving relative poverty untouched.

## Paper 4 style — data response > A fast-growing middle-income country reports: growth averaging 6%; the Gini coefficient up from 0.38 to 0.46 in a decade; a flat 15% income tax; VAT recently raised to 16%; 55% of workers in untaxed informal jobs with no benefits; and top incomes in booming technology and finance sectors. The finance minister argues that 'growth will trickle down eventually'; the opposition proposes progressive income tax bands and universal child benefits. **(a)** Define the 'Gini coefficient'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain what has happened to this country's Lorenz curve over the decade. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse **two** reasons why rapid growth has been accompanied by rising inequality. **[6]** **(d)** Assess the opposition's package of progressive taxation and universal child benefit. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) A summary measure of inequality: the area between the Lorenz curve and the line of equality as a fraction of the whole triangle; 0 = equality, 1 = maximal inequality. [1+1]

(b) It has bowed further from the 45° line — each cumulative slice of the (poorer) population now earns a smaller share of total income. [4]

(c) (i) Skill-biased growth: technology/finance raise top MRPs while informal workers' productivity stagnates; (ii) a regressive tax mix: flat income tax + higher VAT redistributes nothing and taxes the poor's consumption; informality also excludes most workers from any safety net. Any two developed. [3+3]

(d) For: progressive bands introduce vertical equity where none exists; universal child benefit reaches informal families that means-testing would miss (low take-up, no records). Against: narrow formal tax base → high rates on few payers → evasion/emigration risk; universal benefits costly; better paired with formalisation incentives and education spending (causes). Judgement: direction right; design and financing decide success. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain how the Lorenz curve and Gini coefficient are used to measure income inequality. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether government attempts to redistribute income necessarily reduce economic efficiency. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Construction of the curve (cumulative shares, ranked poorest first), the 45° benchmark, Gini = A/(A+B), 0–1 interpretation, income-vs-wealth note.

(b) For the trade-off: high marginal taxes blunt work/saving/enterprise; benefit withdrawal creates poverty traps; capital flight. Against: modest redistribution shows small measured incentive effects; education/health transfers raise efficiency (human capital); extreme inequality itself wastes talent and fuels instability. Judgement: a trade-off exists at the extremes, not necessarily at the margin — design matters more than scale.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain the difference between absolute and relative poverty, and how a poverty trap can arise. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss which policies are most effective in reducing poverty in the long run. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Basic-needs line vs share-of-median; growth ends the first, not automatically the second; trap = benefits withdrawn + tax as earnings rise → near-100% effective marginal rate.

(b) Compare: transfers (immediate relief, but symptom-level and trap-prone), minimum wages (helps working poor; employment caveats), progressive tax funding, **education/training/health** (attacks causes, compounding returns, slow), growth itself (necessary but insufficient without distribution). Judgement: human-capital + trap-free benefit design as the long-run core, transfers as the bridge.
